title: Exhibits
text:
How human-computer interaction evolved from mechanical keys to gesture and voice. Highlights include an 1874 Sholes & Glidden, a Xerox Alto, and the first commercial trackpad.



Seating as a design problem: bentwood, tubular steel, injection-molded plastic, and the ergonomic office chair. Objects from Thonet, Breuer, Eames, and Herman Miller.



Vacuum tubes, transistors, and the first integrated circuits, traced through to the microprocessors that put a computer on every desk.



Toasters, washing machines, and thermostats: the quiet redesign of domestic life, and what it reveals about who that redesign was for.



A hands-on gallery of dials, switches, sliders, and touchscreens spanning seventy years of trying to make machines easier to operate.

title: Exhibits
text:
Rotating exhibition
“Prototypes That Almost Were” — a look at design dead ends: the products that shaped what came after them without ever reaching a shop shelf. On view through the end of the season.
This is a link to the Museum of Modern Art’s exhibition page for “Prototypes That Almost Were.”

title: Visit
text:
Location
Werkbank Museum Gestaltungsweg 12 3000 Bern, Switzerland

title: Visit
text:
Getting there
Tram 9, stop “Werkbank” (5 min walk). Limited on-site parking; public transport is recommended.

title: About
text: The Werkbank Museum collects, preserves, and exhibits the objects of design and technology that shaped how people work, communicate, and live. We believe everyday objects deserve the same close looking as fine art: every switch, seat, and circuit board embeds a decision about how it should feel to be human.

title: About
text: The Werkbank Museum collects, preserves, and exhibits the objects of design and technology that shaped how people work, communicate, and live. We believe everyday objects deserve the same close looking as fine art: every switch, seat, and circuit board embeds a decision about how it should feel to be human.

title: About
text:
History
The museum grew out of a private collection of typewriters and drafting tools assembled by engineer and design historian Elsa Reinhardt starting in the 1970s. What began as a personal archive in a converted workshop (“Werkbank” is German for workbench) opened to the public in 1991 and has since expanded to cover computing, furniture, and household technology.

title: About
text:
Visiting
For hours, admission, and directions, see the Visit page.

title: Werkbank Museum of Design & Technology
text: The Werkbank Museum traces the history of design and technology through the everyday objects that shaped it: typewriters, circuit boards, chairs, telephones, and the machines that made them. Come see how good design solves problems you didn’t know you had.

title: Werkbank Museum of Design & Technology
text: The Werkbank Museum traces the history of design and technology through the everyday objects that shaped it: typewriters, circuit boards, chairs, telephones, and the machines that made them. Come see how good design solves problems you didn’t know you had.

title: Werkbank Museum of Design & Technology
text:
Highlights from the collection


Typewriters to Trackpads
A century of how we’ve talked to machines, and how they’ve talked back.


The Chair as Machine
Thonet to Aeron: seating as an engineering problem.


Circuits & Silicon
From vacuum tubes to the first microprocessors, under glass.


See the full collection →
